item.content:encoded: Well I'll be damned.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sure love it when an article is so abrupt, you say there just has to be more to it, and it turns out there really really is. Great job on riding the line between familiar and obscure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why not just cover it in paint instead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This might be a really dumb question, but if "SCP-3693 occupies space and can be acted upon physically", is it possible to cover it in something like powdered chalk to see what is looks like physically, instead of relying on a sketch made by an observer? Or would SCP-3693's main property extend to the chalk or whatever is used to make its shape visible? I'm curious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I personally play Pale Master.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: djkaktus added the co-authored tag, so all is well! Thanks for confirming.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not djkaktus, but I can confirm that I did indeed write portions of this article with him, and he does indeed consider me a co-author of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think so, if TheGreatHippo co-authored it? djkaktus, can you confirm?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This shouldn't have the co-author tag?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Rare short story! I translated it into Chinese! It's creepy and the interplay between it and 173 is also very enjoyable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Spooky. Totally didn't see the 173 references before I read other comments, but damn is that intriguing. Worth noting that the artist of 173 is Japanese as well…

Very cool, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think its a cool concept, and its very original. Although its short and simple, I think it adds to the mystery of this object. This is a case where less is more.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One look at the tags (…don't ask me why I looked there first) and I could tell that that this thing was related to 173. In any case, this article is the exact kind of infuriatingly vague that I dislike, bashing you over the head with mystery without giving a concrete (no pun intended) story. I do love the imagery of the thing, and all the references are cleverly integrated, but the article just does not do enough to convince me to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: CRUNCHY ISNT A FLAVOR ITS A TEXTURE YOU HOOLIGAN

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Kevin Feige: "Infinity war is the most ambitious crossover event."

Looks at the authors for this article

Kevin Feige: "I stand corrected, THIS is the most ambitious crossover event."

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the simplicity of it. Although, the obvious implied connection to 173 has me on the fence. It conflicted with my headcanon of it but I think I just found a way to incorporate into my headcanon that makes sense. Here is ya +1 for creating another chapter in the headcanon of 173.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's fine, and tbh, I'm surprised it's gotten as many upvotes as it has? I mean I like it, obviously — but I didn't expect it to clear 30. >_>

+ show block
– hide block

There's a story here, but it's kind of subtle and intended to be vague. There's a ghost story about a woman murdered by a jilted lover; a basement where we find the rubble of what might have been multiple sculptures — and the unidentified body of a woman with her neck broken. And then there's a spooky ceramic statue that you can only see when you close your eyes, and follows you when you see it.

The point here is to make the reader wonder: 1) What happened, here? 2) How is this related to 173? 3) What would happen if this thing encountered 173?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm.

There's definitely something going on here that I'm missing. Its related to 173 in some way. Maybe it's just because its 9:00 and I have no business doing any reading at this time, but I really can't make the connection.

I feel like this is too short for its own good. It's really a problem with a lot of the newer short skips that I've found. I don't want to downvote this, but I don't feel like I can confidently upvote this until I can fully wrap my head around it, so I'll just leave a novote until I can come back later.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Articles that are this well and so short are my absolute favorite. You have done a wonderful job portraying a creepy object with ties to the infamous sculpture.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
BRB making 173 x 3693 ship art

For real though, I'm glad short form is becoming popular because not everything needs a long story to be good.

And that picture was enough to seal my upvote. Reminds me of take on me and JJBA

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I get that this is supposed to be 173's antithesis, but I don't see if this is supposed to be anything more. To me, this article feels like it's trying to be clever without being meaningful. The realization hit like a truck, though, so I'll give you that.

No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very interesting reversal. I like it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like to call 173 Crunchy. I hereby propose we nickname this statue 'Salty'. Two great tastes that go great together.

The obvious question here is that if you put these two in a room together, would she contain him, making her Thaumiel? Was that perhaps her intended purpose?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Bleep, I think you meant this old hoot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A brilliant, concise, and creepy article that leaves the implications of its material on the floor in such a manner that it makes my brain leap to fill in the holes of the why's and hows. This is the kind of short article I love, it gives you just enough information, just enough of a narrative to let the readers mind run wild, with a hell of a lot of layered implications about the relationship between the statue, 173, and the implication of some sort of endless punishment for the perpetrator of the crime.

The only real criticism I have for the article, is that the statue itself, while clearly meant to be the polar opposite of 173, doesn't do a whole lot other than creepily stare at people, which reminds me a lot of the mannequinn who appears behind people in vaguely threatening poses. The creep factor alone from the implications, and the clever use of layered, sparsely sprinkled narrative elements are enough to get me to upvote, and upvote heartily, but I can't help but feel like there is something missing from the statue itself to make the item memorable, and not just its narrative.

Otherwise, +1 heartily.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At first I thought "Does 173 need an origin/sequel/companion like this?"

Then the more I thought about it, I realized "If it does, this is how it should be done."

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A shame.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Huh.

That's really all I have to say about this one, honestly. It really culminates in a "huh". It wasn't written badly, but there wasn't really substance, and besides the 173 reference I really don't see the point of this.

Might've just been cause of creep factor, but I didn't find this creepy either. Then again I don't find most stuff creepy in general so I don't think i'm the best judge of that.

-1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's the sequel everyone knew they needed, but nobody could write. Major congratulations, this is everything the wiki has been trying to achieve for ten years.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
We Series I now. :D

Seriously, it's been a long time since I saw an article that was just "thing what is creepy in novel ways" that really worked. But this really works! (Even without the 173 references…)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dammit, beat me to it.
Did it also feel like a freight train?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh. O-Oooooooh. This suddenly makes me feel a lot better about this one.

My novote's become a +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A certain statue, huh?
Ohboyohboy.

From sir fine statue:

It is constructed from concrete and rebar with traces of Krylon brand spray paint.

The reddish brown substance on the floor is a combination of feces and blood. Origin of these materials is unknown. The enclosure must be cleaned on a bi-weekly basis.

Object is reported to attack by snapping the neck at the base of the skull, or by strangulation.

Now for this one:

Further investigation of the site discovered an abundance of crushed concrete debris and twisted rebar, as well as several floor drains clogged with blood and fecal matter. The corpse of a woman with a broken neck was recovered nearby.

Aaand they're both line-of-sight anomalies.

0_0
Well then. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is this a reference to A certain statue?
Close conspiracy theory

an abundance of crushed concrete debris and twisted rebar,

It is constructed from concrete and rebar with traces of Krylon brand spray paint.

as well as several floor drains clogged with blood and fecal matter.

The reddish brown substance on the floor is a combination of feces and blood.

The corpse of a woman with a broken neck was recovered nearby.

Object is reported to attack by snapping the neck at the base of the skull, or by strangulation.


+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This took me an embarrassingly long while to figure out what was going on, but when it finally clicked, damn. This is absolutely brilliant, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The implications are what sold me when this was a draft - this is wonderfully layered in a very small space.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, that did it! Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think your image might be broken; I'm not able to see it.

Try opening the image in a new tab, and then refreshing the tab. You should be able to see the image on the main page after that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think your image might be broken; I'm not able to see it.

I won't worry about that, though. This is a curious little thing.

SCP-3693 can only be seen by observers when their eyes are closed.

This is the line that hooked me. It's such a simple effect, but it's unique. I haven't seen anything in recent memory that had the same kind of effect. And the image it presents, of a floating statue staring you in the face amidst almost total darkness, is cool and slightly unsettling.

I'm unsure why this is Euclid, though. It only reacts when people observe it with their eyes closed, and is otherwise inert. At least, that's what I assumed to be the case. I'm confused why this thing requires constant supervision, and why it isn't Safe. I've read over it a few times, but I can't really begin to come up with an answer.

The discovery log, while mostly benign, did give this thing a little bit more mystery.

After securing SCP-3693 and moving it off-site, Foundation agents returned to the warehouse to continue their examination of the site. The basement could no longer be located.

Curious. I'm not sure what the implications are here, but it does seem like there's a little more to this simple statue than what's in the document.

Gonna novote this one. A little brief and bare for my liking, but I did enjoy the concept and what substance was there. Not enough to upvote, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For now we see through a glass, darkly; but then face to face: now I know in part; but then shall I know even as also I am known.

1 Corinthians 13:12

Thanks to Hippo for cowriting this, and to ARD and Shaggy and whoever else for perusing it and giving comments. Thanks dudes.

Hope ya'll enjoy this very short thing!

Image Credit
